questions.questionText: The most reliable method of personal identification is:
questions.optionB: DNA fingerprinting (STR profiling — unique to each individual except identical twins)
questions.explanation: DNA profiling (DNA fingerprinting): most accurate and reliable method. Uses STR (Short Tandem Repeat) analysis — highly specific (probability of two unrelated individuals having same profile: 1 in billions). Developed by Alec Jeffreys (1984). Can be obtained from: blood, semen, hair root, saliva, bone, teeth. Works on decomposed/skeletal remains. Identical twins: same DNA profile (limitation). Other reliable methods: fingerprints (most common, individualistic — Galton's system), dental records, dactylography. Reference: Reddy — Personal Identification.

questions.questionText: Age estimation in a living person is best done by:
questions.optionB: Dental examination (eruption, dentition) and radiological examination of ossification centers (bone age)
questions.explanation: Age estimation: (1) Dental: most reliable in children (primary eruption: 6 months-2 years, permanent: 6-21 years). Third molar: 17-21 years. Gustafson's method: for adults (attrition, root resorption, cementum, dentin transparency). (2) Radiological (ossification centers): bone age from X-rays. Useful at puberty (olecranon 14-17, iliac crest 14-25). Epiphyseal fusion: reliable age indicators. (3) Secondary sexual characters: pubertal staging. Legal importance: criminal responsibility (7, 12, 18), consent for marriage, employment. Reference: Reddy — Age Estimation.

questions.questionText: Dactylography (fingerprint analysis) is based on the principle that:
questions.optionB: Fingerprint patterns are unique, permanent (from 4th month of fetal life to decomposition), and classifiable
questions.explanation: Dactylography: study of fingerprints for identification. Three principles: (1) Unique: no two persons have identical fingerprint patterns (even identical twins differ), (2) Permanent: formed by 4th month of intrauterine life, persist unchanged until decomposition, (3) Classifiable: can be systematically categorized. Types (Henry's system): loops (60-65% — most common), whorls (30-35%), arches (5%), composites. Galton described minutiae (ridge characteristics — bifurcation, ending, dot, island). Used in: criminal identification, civil identification. Reference: Reddy — Dactylography.
